content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : OPINION REQUEST PARTICIPANTS                    Date : June 28, 1978

                                                     48^[illegible]

From : Barbara Campbell
        FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

Subject: Short, Special Opinion Request Meeting, TODAY, 2:00 p.m.

          Chip Nielsen called this morning about my June 14, 1978,
     letter to Chase Morgan, a copy of which is attached. He
     disagreed without conclusion that the advertisement from
     Citizens Against Government Dumbness was an attempt to
     influence legislative action.

          Chip asked for reconsideration of this advice letter.
     Although there is nothing in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329
     that provides for reconsideration, I told Chip I would discuss
     this matter. The special meeting is necessitated by the June
     30, 1978, deadline for the 650 reports.

          Chip said that he thought that once legislation was passed,
     only administrative action can be influenced. He thought our
     conclusion that the ad was attempting to influence legislative
     action by getting new legislation passed that would change
     SB 1081's provisions and deadlines was too remote and inferential
     from reading the ad. He also said that there was no practical
     way in which an advertisement occurring in May could realistically
     influence legislative action under these facts. He said that
     legislation on such a substantial matter could not have been
     introduced and become effective before the deadline established
     by SB 1081 for the PUC.

          As Chip explained the advertisement, its thrust was to stop
     the gas companies and Winner-Wagner from carrying out its
     "campaign" to contact secretly the various Brown appointees
     who sit on the boards and commissions that have SB1081 responsibi-
     lities. The thought behind the ad was that once the appointees,
     Winner-Wagner and the gas companies saw the ad, their contacts
     would stop. The insertion of the "Contact Governor Brown"
     language was to channel into action the aroused readers.

          Because the ad urged direct communication with Governor
     Brown, we had the "urging other persons to enter into direct
     communication with any elective state official...." required
     for Government Code Section 82045(e). Had the exact ad been
     run without the exhortation to contact Brown, the cost of the
     ad would not have been reportable.

          As a result of the June 14, 1978, letter, we are left with

a situation where the opponent's expenses for an advertisement
will be reported but the money the proponents of SB 1081 have
been spending to influence administrative action will not be.
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18202(a)(2).)

Therefore, I would like to know if:

1. The advice letter should stand;

2. The advice letter should be changed:

   a. Does legislative action have to consist of
an existing piece of legislation?

   b. In our May 12, 1978, letter to Mr. Williams
of the Los Angeles County Bar Association, we
gave the staff opinion that:

...internal discussions of possible legislation,
drafting of proposed language and circulation of
the proposed language for comment to the Association's members are not legislative actions, so
long as there is no communication with legislative
officials regarding specific matters. However,
any direct communication with officials regarding
the introduction or drafting of proposed legislation, whether or not it is ever introduced, will
be considered influencing legislative action....

(Emphasis added.)

The Governor is not a legislative official. The
actions of the Governor are legislative only insofar as approving or vetoing any bill.

Adopt the Williams approach and say it's not
legislative action since it did not urge communication with legislative officials.

   c. Other

_____________

June 29, 1978 - At the Opinion Request meeting on June 28, 1978,
the staff decided to stay with the June 14, 1978, advice letter.
Chip Nielsen said he would file a letter of appeal with the Commissioners.

sections.question_synthetic: Should the FPPC reconsider its June 14, 1978 advice letter concluding that a Citizens Against Government Dumbness advertisement urging contact with Governor Brown constitutes an attempt to influence legislative action under Government Code Section 82045(e)?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Following a June 28, 1978 opinion request meeting, the FPPC staff decided to maintain its original June 14, 1978 advice letter position that the advertisement constitutes an attempt to influence legislative action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC memorandum rather than a typical advice letter response. It documents staff discussion and decision regarding reconsideration of a prior June 14, 1978 advice letter. The document includes the substantive questions being debated but presents them as options for reconsideration rather than as an incoming question. No traditional Question/Conclusion sections are present.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should the FPPC reconsider its June 14, 1978 advice letter concluding that a Citizens Against Government Dumbness advertisement urging contact with Governor Brown constitutes an attempt to influence legislative action under Government Code Section 82045(e)?

CONCLUSION: Following a June 28, 1978 opinion request meeting, the FPPC staff decided to maintain its original June 14, 1978 advice letter position that the advertisement constitutes an attempt to influence legislative action.

embedding.summary: This is an internal FPPC memorandum documenting a reconsideration request regarding whether an advertisement urging communication with Governor Brown to address concerns about SB 1081 implementation constitutes reportable lobbying activity under state law.
